FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study wave 1 release; N=6758 individuals ages 9.94 years baseline

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-validated latent profile analysis (LPA) on fMRI data identifying 7 neurodevelopmental profiles

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper, Biological Psychiatry: Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time mentioned only as a descriptive demographic comparison in Table results ("more screen time F=6.80 p<.0001") across latent profiles, not as IV or DV in an inferential analysis paired with individual-trait outcome

notes: Paper is a neurodevelopmental profiling study. Screen time is only reported as a descriptive variable comparing profile groups; no analysis of screen time as IV or DV related to individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, May 2020 COVID-19 survey, N=5412 adolescents ages 10-14

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis; multiple linear regression models associating screen use modalities with mental health and resiliency factors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper, JAMA Pediatrics 2022 January

FT_IC2.evidence: Total screen time across 7 modalities (texting, social media, video chatting, streaming, multiplayer gaming, single-player gaming, browsing) paired with individual-trait mental health variables: mental health, perceived stress, social support, coping behaviors

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression: poorer mental health B=0.29 (95% CI 0.06-0.52) p=.01; perceived stress B=0.67 (95% CI 0.43-0.91) p<.001; social support B=-0.32 (95% CI -0.59 to -0.04) p=.02; coping behaviors B=-0.17 (95% CI -0.26 to -0.09) p<.001

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results reported: regression β coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for screen time ↔ mental health outcome associations in main text

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional; EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs
notes: Cross-sectional analysis during pandemic. Screen time (IV) associated with mental health outcomes (DV). Includes multiple modalities and individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, N=5335 ages 10-14, COVID-19 survey May 2020

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: parent-adolescent discrepancy study with linear regression models examining predictors of reporting differences

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper, Academic Pediatrics 2022 April

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures parent and adolescent reports of recreational screen time (texting, social media, video chatting, multiplayer gaming) but does NOT analyze associations between screen time and any individual-trait-level outcome variable. Analysis focuses on parent-adolescent reporting discrepancies and sociodemographic predictors of discrepancies, not on screen time paired with behavioral, health, or well-being outcomes

notes: Measurement validation/discrepancy study. No analysis of screen time as IV or DV related to child outcomes. Screen time is the outcome being measured but not analyzed for associations with individual-trait variables.
